---
title: "CUSUM"
format: html
---

CUSUM adds up how far each reading strays from its target. Small shifts that no single reading would catch still pile up, so it spots a slow, steady change early, like the start of an outbreak. Click through the vaccine line below: each vial is measured, and the CUSUM climbs until it signals.

```{=html}
<div id="cusum-game" class="epr-mount"></div>
```

E.S. Page invented CUSUM in 1954 for factory quality control [@page1954]. Tillett and Spencer brought it into disease surveillance in 1982, with weekly influenza "cusum graphs" for England and Wales [@tillett1982]. Page tracked one parameter of a distribution, usually the mean, and set a rule for when a change was big enough to act on.

## Computing an epidemic threshold with CUSUM

For weekly case counts, the standard tool is the CUSUM in R's `surveillance` package (`algo.cusum`) [@rossi1999], building on counted-data CUSUMs [@lucas1985]. It runs in three steps, using the package defaults.

**1. Expected level.** Take the in-control mean $m$, the count you expect in a normal week. By default the package uses the mean of the weeks before monitoring starts.

**2. Standardize.** Turn each count into a standardized deviation,

$$ z_t = \frac{x_t - m}{\sqrt{m}}, $$

so a normal week sits near $z_t \approx 0$ whatever the size of $m$.

**3. Accumulate.** Add up these deviations minus a reference value $k$, floored at zero, and signal once the sum reaches the boundary $h$:

$$ S_t = \max\!\left(0,\; S_{t-1} + (z_t - k)\right), \qquad \text{signal when } S_t \ge h. $$

The defaults are $k = 1.04$ and $h = 2.26$, and $S_t$ is not reset after a signal, so a whole outbreak stays flagged.

The same rule reads as a threshold on the raw count. A week signals exactly when its cases reach

$$ x_t \ge U_t = \left\lceil m + \sqrt{m}\,(h + k - S_{t-1}) \right\rceil, $$

the package's `upperbound`. This $U_t$ is the **epidemic threshold in cases**. It starts high and falls as evidence builds, so a steady rise is caught even when no single week looks extreme. Hover across the plot below to see each week's numbers.

```{=html}
<div id="cusum-epi" class="epr-mount"></div>
```

```{=html}
<script src="js/epr-viz.js"></script>
<script src="js/cusum.js"></script>
<script src="js/cusum-epi.js"></script>
```
